speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we lived for six years in Switzerland. At first, I didn't like it at all. It was, again, a different county. And I had settled down in England very well. But life was much easier there. They were much more advanced with mother's help things. And so it was actually quite good that we lived there while I had the two little children. But physically, I was rather exhausted. And I couldn't-- as I say, I wanted really about six, seven children. I felt I had to give it a break. And so Sylvia was born after a four-year gap.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was born in a Swiss hospital, which was very backwards to English ways already at the time. The child-- the baby was not allowed to stay with the mother in the room. And you just had 10 minutes to feed. And wrapped up-- before I took her home, I didn't even see that she had a sound body. They never unwrapped her. You know, you just got a bundle to feed, and it was taken away, very Victorian. So that was in '62, still like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we really wanted to come back to England. But you know how jobs are is let's stay another six months and another year and another. And so it was six years. And then he could have become manager there or go back. And we discussed it, and I said, well, I don't really want the children to grow up Swiss. And because in Switzerland-- so it was quite nice living. They hadn't gone through a war. And everything had to be just right, you know. They were not compromising with-- they were-- they were very difficult to-- to please, you know? And we lived in a little house, but then had always arguments with washing machines or with this and that, you know. And you had to be out by this time. And it was a very, very strict way, not very pleasant. So we decided to come back. But after it come back, I felt it was difficult again, because it was much easier, really, living there. So it was again a difficult period.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for the children, they went really to an American school. I mean, Caroline was seven years old. She was seven to eight. So she had to go here to real school. But it was not a language problem. And-- but all this time, I'd really not told them anything about what had happened, about war, or who we were, with Auschwitz, or anything like that. But Otto, of course, talked a lot about Anne. And I mean, they were too small to understand about the diary, but Caroline is called Caroline Anne, after Anne. And Otto, because she looked a bit-- she was dark hair and big dark eyes, and very often-- and she was then six, seven years old, when he remembered Anne. And very often, he called her Anne. And for the children, Otto was really their grandfather. They didn't know anybody else. I wasn't-- I learned a lot about Anne through Otto, because he talked. This was his life and my mother too. They built up a new life together around Anne, really. And I learned a lot about her, how she was, which I hadn't really known, because I hadn't known her too well in Amsterdam. She was just one of the friends I played with. And not one of the best friends, because she was already a bit more grown up than I. She was interested in film stuff and boys already then. And I was just interested in marbles and skipping and things like that. Like, you know, I didn't-- like, she started to write her diary, and I was hardly reading books at all at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was, of course, nice that we lived in Switzerland, because we could visit quite often. And then, when we moved back to England, my parents built here a little extension, so that they would have a separate cloak room. And the downstairs home was made it into a room where they could have a bed and work. So they came quite a lot here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was always a little jealous when they came here. My mother was not always allowed to be with me. She had to first write letters with Otto. So we had a bit of arguments. And so I don't want to say anything bad about Otto. He was the most fantastic man, and the most kind man, and the most understanding person. And he tried to be the best father and grandfather. I still had a resentment against him, because my father was, for me, unique. And it was my father who was living with my mother. And that is something I-- I couldn't-- you know, I couldn't get over. So I'm afraid-- I think I did hurt him quite a bit, when I didn't always accept him as a father. So I must say he did accept me as a daughter. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I really wanted to have more children, but it was-- it was difficult because Zvi didn't really take part in the-- he became manager of Bank Leumi England. And he traveled a lot to mainly Israel, but as well to America and South Africa and all kind of things. He was a very, very-- originally German-- but a very conscientious worker. He really worked from 6 o'clock in the morning till 10 o'clock at night. So he didn't really help me with the children. Then he didn't drive a car, so I had to, you know, with three children, take them to different classes after school and everything. And I was-- physically it was hard. But we still-- we did get another child. And by that time, I had developed colitis, which is an illness of the intestines. And I was at times quite ill, and when I was pregnant again, that was-- Caroline was 13 at the time. So the doctor said I should have a check up at the-- for colitis-- hospital and see if it was advisable. And I went to-- I went to this hospital. I had an examination. And they said they were going to take results and send me results. But in the meantime, I went to a gynecologist-- a private gynecologist-- as well to have a checkup. And he gave me a terrible internal examination, and then that night I had a-- how do you call it-- miscarriage. And it was just three of us in Israel. And I was alone in the house with the children. And it was very traumatic for the whole family, actually. And then, after a few days, the hospital wrote that could I have a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I was very, very upset. And I tried to sue this doctor. I went to my-- my GP. And I said, look, he shouldn't have had the examination. But you know, they protected each other, and nothing came of it. And that was the end of the family, though that was actually quite sad. And especially, it was-- it was a boy. So that's something which [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] felt me-- made me feel quite sad. But then, the children grew up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were, must say, sweet daughters. They were all very nice children. And they all turned out well. So you know, we had the normal problems, but nothing special. And Jackie, the middle one, was the first to get married. She was not so academic as the others. But she's the most practical of them. And I think she's the most like me. She knows what she wants, and she goes. She is determined. And she does it. She wanted to be a beautician and a beauty therapist and she, after her A level, she did that. She went and had a three-year course. And then she-- she was really good. She worked in Gleneagles Hotel. And then she decided to go on a boat, on a Norwegian cruise ship where she met her husband, a Norwegian [NORWEGIAN] Hovelson. That's why the children look rather Scandinavian. Unfortunately, he was the ship's engineer. And she didn't want him to stay on the ship. And so they decided to come and live here. But he wasn't happy here, and he started drinking, and it ended up in a divorce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they tried to save the marriage. They had one little girl, Lisa. They tried to save the marriage getting another child. So by the time the boy was born, he had already-- they were already separated. So I was present-- I was present at the birth. And they stayed here for two years with us. So I'm very close to Jackie and her children. And I feel really more as if they are my children. I very often used to say to Lisa, come to Mom, you know, because they grew up with me. So you know, that helped me a bit over that I didn't have any more children. My grandchildren were worth a lot to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Caroline got married and had a little boy. And her husband is an Orthodox Jew. And the family is rather orthodox. And Alex is very religious. He's only four, but he knows all the prayers. He goes to a Jewish school. Jackie's children, of course-- so their father, of course, was not Jewish, as their mother is Jewish, they are Jews, and they go to synagogue and to religious classes. And Eric will be bar mitzvah. And then, the last one, Sylvia, met Gil. Sylvia wanted to emigrate to Israel, which pleased my husband immensely. And she went on aliyah, and she went on kibbutz. And she learned Hebrew. And she was out for three years. And then she was going to stay there. She was a journalist. And then she came back to get all her immigration goods, her pictures and videos and-- and television, because you can take this in at first. And she was in a-- in such a electrical shop in Holborn. And there was an Israeli family there who, she said, behaved terrible, demanding this and that and this. And then she said, no, I don't want to go to Israel. And she came home and said, I changed my mind. I'm not going to emigrate. [LAUGHS] I was delighted. So Zvi was rather upset. But she still had to go back, because she left everything. She had just come here on holiday. And on the plane, she met a Jewish boy who went visiting his uncle there. And his family was from Kenton. And they got to know each other. First, he stayed there, and then-- then she came back. And that's the boy she married. So that was a great coincidence. And he's an economist. And he travels a lot. And they went for two years to Namibia. And they got married after that child was already eight months. But now they've got another baby. And they're all very happy. So for me to see all this, that's a great satisfaction now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- what do you do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave up photography long ago. I still worked first for another studio which was color photography. And it was very difficult work, but I enjoyed that. But then-- then after I had the children, I didn't want to go back into photography. And I realized it wasn't really what I liked. And I started to go to antique restoration classes. And then I started to deal a bit. And then a shop came empty really around the corner of me. And I opened up an antique business, since 1972. And I'm still learning that. And Sylvia was still quite young then. She was just 10 years old. So I wasn't always available to her. But she said, no, she likes it, actually, because I'm-- I'm always interesting. I can always talk about something that happened in the shop. And she says when she comes to friends their mothers pounce on them and ask them to tell me what had happened in school, because in '60 '70 it wasn't so common yet that mothers worked. So they were quite satisfied that I wasn't always at home and interfering with their little lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that all worked out very well, actually. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we traveled a lot then, as well, with the children. We went every year to Israel. The children knew Israel. That's why Sylvia was actually quite interested. But the others weren't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they're all not religious. But Caroline just fell in love with a religious boy. So they're all-- all a bit different. And all living near us, so we see each other quite regularly. My only problem at the moment is really my mother who is 91 years old and still lives in Switzerland. When Otto died in 1980, we thought she would come and live here. But she didn't want to, because she had all her friends there, and she had lived 28 years happily there with Otto and she lived in a house where she had-- surrounded with all her things, her remembrance of Otto, and she did want to. And now, it's a bit difficult. She doesn't really know what she wants. And I go over to Switzerland now about every month to see her. But she wants to be the family. But she was here for eight months. But then she wanted to go back again. She wanted to be in her house. So this is really a problem at some moment, especially because I was so very close with my mother. People say, well, she should go in a home here. But I'm reluctant to do this. But we'll have to see how it will end up. But this is-- at the moment that is really my only worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all in all, I think, after having gone through this nightmare, I think, you know, I can cope with life. And I think I came out really stronger than I was before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You went back to Holland after the war. Did you go back to Vienna at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've just been-- I went once with Sylvia when she was about 16, 17. We went for a few days. But we were not particularly keen. We were not impressed. We felt strange there. But I went just now there to open an Anne Frank exhibition. And this time we were there for four days. And I felt quite at home there, and I could cope with population, and I quite enjoyed my stay there. I'm very active with the Anne Frank exhibition. This is an exhibition about Anne's life. And it travels around the whole world. It has been in Russia and in Chile and in South Africa in Japan. All the countries where there's problem, It's very important-- racial problems. And it's an educational exhibition. Mainly school parties go there. And they are taught about the life of Anne as an example, that it's not particularly more of-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] if it's one child, they can cope with that, not if you say 6 million people got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's a great success, this exhibition. And now for-- after having traveled for 10 years, they have a new version of the exhibition. And that was the world opening in Vienna. And it was high-profile. The German ambassador and Dutch ambassador and a representative of the Vatican was there. It was-- and a lot of members of parliament all being sorry what had happened in Austria, and it shouldn't happen again, because Austria till now hasn't really admitted too much of their guilt.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- I started to speak for the very first really about what had happened to me in 1985, when this exhibition came to England. And it was opening in-- in the mall, there is an exhibition hall. And it was the first time this exhibition had been shown. And it was a lot of press, and a lot of important people were there, MPs. And Ken Livingston was the chairman. And my mother was here, and I went just as one guest. And when we got there, Ken Livingston said I think you should sit on the platform. And so I did, still not suspecting anything. And then everybody talked, and there were questions from the press and everything. And then suddenly, Ken Livingston said, and I think now Eva will want to say a few words to you. And I had never, ever spoken in public and never ever about anything in connection with that. And first minute, I was quite in shock, didn't know what to say. But then, suddenly, it opened up. And I talked quite a bit. And everybody was-- it was the first time people had heard about things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my daughters were there with a lot of their friends. And afterwards, they all came to me and hundreds of questions and wanted to know and-- and I realized suddenly that people are interested and people do want to know about it. And this was really a big turning point in my life and in all this, because from then on, I spoke to nearly-- at every exhibition, I spoke, prepared then, not so, you know, just from openly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then people started to ask me to speak in schools. Teachers asked me. And when I realized how important it was and the reaction of the children-- ask and were interested, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did that. And this changed my life, as well, because I'm partner of a group of people who tell about the story. And it is important, because, like my mother now unfortunately is not able to give testimony anymore. And so there are thousands of testimonies-- it's still, compared-- to the millions of people-- it's still only a very small part of the victims.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever written anything about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1988, I decided to write a book about my experience, again, because people asked me. They were interested and said, you have so much to tell. Why don't you write about it. And it took three years. And it became very, I think, very interesting. It's used a lot in schools. It's been translated. Well, it's written in English and then translated in Dutch, German. It came out in America, Norway, Sweden, Japan. And it sold many thousands of copies. And in America, now they're making a film. Part-- no, it's really a play, a play with video about my story, because-- and starting in New Jersey-- because in America it's compulsory now to teach about the Holocaust. So this is just happening now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for talking to us this afternoon about your experiences, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for taking your-- your time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, you're showing us your tattoo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say the number, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: A5272. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Most people have it bit like this and bit like this and much stronger. But this my mother said, do it small. So she was kind and really did it lightly and small. And then other people have the whole thing scratched out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you keep your hand-- that's it. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and a new number put on top. With me, it just happened to be one number without a sequel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you thought about having it removed at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of people in Holland did have it removed-- a skin graft. But we said, well, there's nothing-- we don't have to be ashamed of it. So we kept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this you have, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when we left Auschwitz. My mother said, well, you have to carry all your belongings. So she got a piece of rag and sewed me a rucksack, which I kept all those years. You can see it's in not very good condition. But they did properly the straps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you turn the other side? And what did you carry in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some food and clothes and was very full with, uh, bits perhaps, spoons, and everything went in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And behind it, what is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when the Russians fitted us out with clothes. All they had was their uniforms, so they gave us all their uniforms. There was a skirt as well. And you can still see the buttons of the Red Army. Doesn't fit me anymore. That was when I was very skinny. But-- and a belt we had. We were very proud walking around like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us the back? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was the nice part-- the collar?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were proud of that. This is the house where we lived when I-- where I-- well, I wasn't born in the house, but where we lived as small children. And I don't know exactly anymore where it was in Vienna. And that was a big garden where we played this wagon and where the garage was. And we moved out here about 1934 when my father lost his business for a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- can you say the dates, approximately, when you lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- well I lived there from 1929 till about 1934. This is a photograph taken after I was born only a few months. It's my mother, [? Fritzy ?] Geiringer, with Heinz standing next to her and me in her arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: At a studio in Vienna. This was taken after we moved to our other house in [NON-ENGLISH], and it was with a little boy who lived around the corner, Martin Hahn. And it's me and only this year I've been in contact with him again after 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it must've been 1935. I think I was six, and he was four. This was taken in Brussels. That's-- this little girl is called Kitty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which little girl is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The one on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left. That's Kitty, which was a little girlfriend of my brother Heinz, who's standing-- who's walking next to her. And next to it is a little boy called Jackie. He's a-- was a French boy. He was the son of the owner of the boarding house where we lived. And I had sort of a crush on him. And that's why I called one of my door daughters Jackie. And next to it is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're right on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is at a fancy dress party, where I went with the son of the owner of the boarding house, Jackie. I don't know anymore his second name. And the clothes were bought by those German refugee Dutch who lived in the boarding house who liked us as children, and they didn't have any children. So they bought beautiful clothes for us to go to this fancy dress party.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Brussels, and it must have been 1939.
